query: Is Ixodes a genus of tick?

retrieved_docs.title: Tick Removal -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Ticks have multiple life stages and must eat blood at every stage to survive. Not all ticks bite humans, and not all ticks that bite humans transmit disease. In the contiguous United States, there are 7 commonly encountered ticks that bite humans and are known to transmit disease, each with a unique geographical distribution. [2] These ticks are: American dog tick ( Dermacentor variabilis and Dermacentor similis ) [3] Blacklegged tick ( Ixodes scapularis ) [4] Brown dog tick ( Rhipicephalus sanguineus sensu lato) Gulf Coast tick ( Amblyomma maculatum ) Lone star tick ( Amblyomma americanum ) [5] Rocky Mountain wood tick ( Dermacentor andersoni ) Western blacklegged tick ( Ixodes pacificus )

retrieved_docs.title: Tick Removal -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The Western blacklegged tick ( I pacificus ) is found along the United States Pacific coast, Nevada, Utah, and Arizona. [CDC, Tick Surveillance] I pacificus transmits  several human pathogens, including Borrelia burgdorferi, a causative agent of Lyme disease; Anaplasma phagocytophilum, which causes anaplasmosis;  Bartonella, and Rickettsiales . Various host species, including small mammals, birds, livestock, and domestic animals, sustain these pathogens within the environment. [20] Larvae and nymphs often feed on lizards, birds, and rodents; adults usually feed on deer. Nymphs and adult females are more frequently reported to bite humans than other life stages of these ticks.[CDC, Regions Where Ticks Live]

retrieved_docs.title: Insect Bites -- Etiology -- Insecta
retrieved_docs.content: The most significant impact of ticks on humans is their ability to serve as vectors for significant diseases including Rocky Mountain spotted fever, endemic typhus, ehrlichiosis, Q-fever, encephalitis, hemorrhagic fever, Lyme disease, relapsing fever, tularemia, babesiosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Tick Removal -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Ticks are arachnid ectoparasites of the order Ixodida. They are small, hematophagic parasites that feed on the blood of animals, including humans. Ticks carry many bacterial, viral, and protozoal diseases that can affect humans; the number of such conditions continues to grow. Additionally, the geographical range of many ticks is expanding. As ticks are a very important vector of disease-causing pathogens in humans, tick removal requires awareness beyond the physical act of removing the tick from the patient. Clinicians should consider the type of tick, the geographic distribution of ticks in general, and any need for further therapies. This activity summarizes information about tick identification, the technique of removing attached ticks, and information about prophylaxis against some tick-borne diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: Insect Bites -- Etiology -- Insecta
retrieved_docs.content: Nymphal and adult ticks are characterized by the presence of eight legs tipped with a pair of claws and an oval-shaped body which becomes engorged during feeding. Most ticks are categorized as hard ticks, belong to the Ixodidae family, or soft ticks belonging to the Argasidae family. Ticks feed by cutting a hole in the epidermis and injecting anticoagulants or compounds which inhibit platelet aggregation. Tick bites are usually painless and can present with a wide variety of rashes and other dermatologic findings, making diagnosis challenging. Bites often appear as an erythematous papule with surrounding erythema while others may present as pruritic urticarial lesions. Tick-borne infectious diseases such as Lyme disease and Rocky Mountain spotted fever present with characteristic rashes; however, these are not always identified.

retrieved_docs.title: Tick Removal -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Ticks are arachnid ectoparasites of the order Ixodida. [1] They are small, hematophagic parasites that feed on the blood of animals, including humans. Finding a tick on oneself or a family member can be anxiety provoking. Ticks carry many bacterial, viral, and protozoal diseases that can affect humans; the number of such conditions continues to grow. Additionally, the geographical range of many ticks is expanding, possibly partly due to climate change.

retrieved_docs.title: Anaplasma Phagocytophilum -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Anaplasmosis is found worldwide, particularly in the northeast United States, northern Europe, and Southeast Asia (has been reported in China, Mongolia, and Korea). The disease is transmitted by the bites of nymphal or adult Ixodes ticks. The species varies according to the region where the infection is transmitted. In the Eastern and the Midwestern United States, the vector is the Ixodes scapularis tick commonly known as the black-legged or deer tick; whereas in the Western United States, the tick vector is Ixodes pacificus. Other important tick vectors are Ixodes ricinus in West Europe and Ixodes persulcatus in Asia. It is important to point out that Ixodes ticks are commonly coinfected with other organisms and can transmit Lyme disease, Babesia, Ehrlichia, Rickettsia, and Powassan virus.

retrieved_docs.title: Lyme Disease -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The tick lifecycle is significant to understanding when they are infectious. They have a 2- to 3-year life span and cycle through 4 life stages: egg, larva, nymph, and adult. The larva and nymph must take a blood meal to advance to the next life stage, and the female ticks require blood to reproduce. During these meals, ticks become infected with the bacteria. Ticks attach to an animal host reservoir, typically birds or other small mammals such as mice and voles, and feed anywhere from 3 to 7 days. While ticks can feed on any mammal, deer are not a natural reservoir of the spirochete. Those infected nymphs and adult ticks spread the bacteria each time they subsequently feed.

retrieved_docs.title: Tick Removal -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Brown dog ticks ( R sanguineus ) are the most widespread ticks globally and are well-recognized vectors of many pathogens affecting dogs and occasionally humans. [10] [11] At least two lineages of R sanguineus are present in the United States. [12] Dogs are the primary host for the brown dog tick in each life stage, but the tick may also bite humans or other mammals. Brown dog ticks are vectors of Rickettsia rickettsii that cause Rocky Mountain spotted fever from the southwestern United States into northeastern Mexico. [13] [14] The brown dog tick is a vector of Rickettsia conorii that causes Meditteranean spotted fever in the Meditteranean basin. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Insect Bites -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Ticks: Lyme disease, Rocky Mountain spotted fever, relapsing fever, anaplasmosis, babesiosis, tularemia Flies: tularemia, leishmaniasis, African trypanosomiasis, bartonellosis, loiasis Fleas: plague, tularemia, murine typhus Chigger mites: scrub typhus (predominantly Asia, not in the US) Body lice: epidemic typhus, relapsing fever Kissing bugs: Chagas disease Mosquitoes: malaria, yellow fever, dengue fever, West Nile virus, equine encephalitis, chikungunya, Zika virus

retrieved_docs.title: Borrelia Burgdorferi -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: This type of spirochete is a tick-borne obligate parasite whose preferred naural reservoir is small mammals and birds; it does not cause disease in these natural hosts. When the parasite infects a human, Lyme borreliosis or Lyme disease may develop. The main route of dissemination for B. burgdorferi is through the tick species Ixodes scapularis , more commonly known as the black-legged tick. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Tick Removal -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Tick removal requires awareness beyond the physical act of removing the tick from the patient. Clinicians should consider the type of tick, the geographic distribution of ticks in general, and any need for further therapies. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) is an excellent resource for all clinicians caring for patients with tick bites.

retrieved_docs.title: Powassan Virus -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Transmission to humans occurs primarily through the bite of an infected tick. Three main species of ticks have been identified as vectors for transmitting the POW virus, but Ixodes scapularis (deer tick) is the primary vector. [9] [2] Ixodes scapularis is also a vector for other common pathogens that cause human infection, including Borrelia burgdorferi , Babesia microti , and Anaplasma phagocytophilum . Ixodes cookei (groundhog tick) and Ixodes marxi (squirrel tick) can also transmit the POW virus but rarely bite humans. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Relapsing Fever -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Humans and many other vertebrate species may be hosts for TBRF Borrelia species. Typical vectors include the Ornithodorids for humans, Argasidae for birds, and Rhipicephalus for ruminants (eg, bovine borreliosis). Rodents are the most common reservoir following vector transmission, but humans and other vertebrates can become infected. Tick vectors may also be considered reservoirs for Borrelia due to their transovarial and transstadial spirochete transmission through various generations of progeny. [18] Gravid ticks infected with Borrelia will lay multiple larvae, which progress and mature from nymphs to mating adults, which can propagate infection. In this way, Borrelia spirochetes may be epidemiologically dormant until their exposure to susceptible hosts following the next feed.

retrieved_docs.title: Ehrlichiosis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: E chaffeensis , E canis , and E muris are intracellular coccobacilli with a tropism for monocytes and macrophages in their hosts. E chaffeensis , which was initially identified in 1986, is the cause of human monocytic ehrlichiosis, the most common ehrlichiosis in North America and the most severe disease out of all the ehrlichioses. [9] [10] The reservoir for E chaffeensis is the white-tailed deer ( Odocoileus virginianus ),  and the disease is transmitted by the lone star tick ( Amblyomma americanum ) and, more rarely, by other ticks, such as the  American dog tick ( Dermacentor variabilis ). E ewingii is also transmitted by the lone star tick ( A americanum ). [6] E canis mostly infects dogs but is also known to affect humans and is transmitted by the brown dog tick  ( Rhipicephalus sanguineus ). [9] [11] E muris– like agent was first identified as the cause of human infections in Wisconsin and Minnesota in 2009 and is transmitted in the United States by Ixodes cookei and I scapularis ticks, by Haemaphysalis flava ticks in Japan, by I persulcatu s ticks in Eastern Europe, and by I ricinus ticks in Western Europe. [6] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Lyme Disease -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: In the US, Lyme disease is caused by the bacterial spirochete Borrelia burgdorferi ( and rarely Borrelia mayonii) and is transmitted by the bite of an Ixodes genus black-legged tick. In Europe and Asia, the predominant causes of Lyme disease are B burgdorferi , Borrelia afzelii , and Borrelia garinii . [3] [4] In Asia, B garinii is the most common cause of Lyme disease. B burgdorferi has a particular affinity for joints, while B garinii has a predilection for nervous system tissue. B afzelii has an affinity for the skin. In the US, Borrelia spp are transmitted by ticks, with Ixodes scapularis being the main vector in the Northeastern and upper Midwest regions and Ixodes pacificus in the Western region. Ixodes ricinus and Ixodes persulcatus are the main species in Europe and Asia, respectively.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2910
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is a term that can be applied in the context of arbovirus research and also within immunology. Immunology, in turn, is relevant to a range of conditions and stages, including the developmental phase of puberty (البلوغ), the plasma‑cell malignancy multiple myeloma, and the parasitic infestation head lice (سعفة الرأس). These links illustrate that immunological considerations span developmental biology, hematologic oncology, and infectious disease, and that the same qualifying language used for arboviral topics may be appropriately employed when describing immunological aspects of these diverse medical entities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1590
retrieved_docs.content: Other infections, including parasitic infections, are classified as a subcategory under the broader parent disease group داء الريكتسيات.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2434
retrieved_docs.content: Other ectoparasiticides, including scabicides in ATC, are classified as a subset of ectoparasiticides, including scabicides.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1573
retrieved_docs.content: Oxytetracyklin may be used to treat داء الريكتسيات, a rickettsial disease that is classified under the broader category of other infections; including parasitic infections. Recognizing داء الريكتسيات as part of this infection group helps clinicians understand its etiological context and supports the selection of appropriate antimicrobial therapy such as oxytetracyklin for effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2263
retrieved_docs.content: Navigational concept is classified as an arthropod‑borne viral encephalitis (التهاب الدماغ الفيروسي المنقول بالمفصليات) and is also linked to immune system disorders (اضطراب الجهاز المناعي), indicating that this condition both belongs to the group of viral encephalitides transmitted by arthropods and falls within the broader category of immune dysregulation. This overlap highlights the interplay between infectious encephalitic processes and immune system pathology, which is important for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2638
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIPARASITIC PRODUCTS, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS is a broad therapeutic class that encompasses ECTOPARASITICIDES, INCL. SCABICIDES, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS; the latter represent a specific subgroup of ant parasitic agents that target ectoparasites and include scabicides, insecticides, and repellents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2639
retrieved_docs.content: Sulfur containing product ectoparasiticides are classified as a type of ectoparasiticides, a group that also includes scabicides. As sulfur‑based agents, these ectoparasiticides are employed to treat infestations caused by external parasites such as mites, lice, and scabies‑causing organisms, leveraging sulfur’s antiparasitic properties to eliminate or control the offending organisms and alleviate associated skin irritation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 555
retrieved_docs.content: Phenothrin is a synthetic pyrethroid that belongs to the broader class of pyrethrines, a group of synthetic compounds commonly employed as ectoparasiticides and scabicides. As a member of the pyrethrines, phenothrin functions as a pesticide, targeting external parasites such as lice, mites, and the organisms that cause scabies. This classification highlights phenothrin’s role within the pyrethrine family of ectoparasiticides and underscores its utility in pest control and the treatment of parasitic skin conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 471
retrieved_docs.content: to the amphenicol class, is incorporated into numerous combination products, and is situated within the systemic antibacterial drug taxonomy alongside aminoglycosides, macrolides, lincosamides, and streptogramins. Its clinical relevance includes the treatment of rickettsial diseases, reflecting its role in managing infections that fall under the broader category of parasitic and other atypical infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1501
retrieved_docs.content: Quinoline derivatives and related substances are classified as antitrematodals, indicating that they belong to the drug class used to treat infections caused by trematode parasites.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2841
retrieved_docs.content: In parasitology, the qualifier “Allowed qualifier” is applicable to both قيلة مثانية and قيلة مستقيمية, indicating that these two entities are recognized and can be described using this permitted descriptor. Conversely, the term “Without” is not associated with either قيلة مثانية or قيلة مستقيمية, meaning that no relationship of “associated with” exists between “Without” and these two entities. This delineation clarifies that while the allowed qualifier is relevant to both قيلة مثانية and قيلة مستقيمية within the parasitological context, the concept “Without” does not pertain to them.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2064
retrieved_docs.content: Oxytetracyclin is an antimicrobial agent whose physiologic effect includes a reduction in cellular mitosis, reflecting its impact on rapidly dividing cells. This drug may be employed in the treatment of داء الريكتسيات (Rickettsial disease), an infection that is classified under the broader category of “other infections; including parasitic.” Recognizing that Rickettsial disease belongs to this group of parasitic‑related infections helps clinicians understand the therapeutic relevance of oxytetracyclin, both for its antimicrobial activity against the pathogen and for its cellular effects that may influence treatment considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1749
retrieved_docs.content: Quinoline derivatives and related substances, antitrematodals are classified as antitrematodal agents, indicating that they belong to the therapeutic class used to treat trematode infections. These compounds are also regarded as members of the praziquantel group, linking them to the well‑known antiparasitic drug praziquantel and highlighting their role within this broader category of anti‑trematode medications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1293
retrieved_docs.content: Oxytetracyklin is an antibiotic that exerts a physiologic effect of decreasing mitosis, reflecting its impact on cellular proliferation. This property, together with its antimicrobial activity, makes oxytetracyklin a therapeutic option for treating داء الريكتسيات (rickettsial disease). Understanding both the antiproliferative effect and the antimicrobial efficacy of oxytetracyklin informs its appropriate use in managing infections caused by rickettsial organisms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2751
retrieved_docs.content: Quinoline derivatives and related substances that function as antitrematodals are classified as members of the praziquantel group, indicating that these quinoline‑based compounds share the pharmacologic characteristics of praziquantel and are used in the treatment of trematode infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1400
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIPARASITIC PRODUCTS, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS represents a broad therapeutic category that includes the more specific group ECTOPARASITICIDES, INCL. SCABICIDES, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS. This latter group is itself a subclass of the broader class ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides. Within the ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides category, the ATC‑defined subgroup Other ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides in ATC is a distinct subclass. Together, these entities form a hierarchical classification in which anti‑parasitic products and repellents encompass ectoparasiticides that contain scabicides, insecticides and repellents, and the ATC “Other ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides” are a specific subset of that broader ectoparasiticide class.
